topStories.headline: Fisherman's Wharf New Public Plaza on Track for Summer Opening as Port Seeks Input on Long-Term Resilience Plan
topStories.dek: The $10 million Fisherman's Wharf Forward project is nearing completion of its new Taylor Street plaza this summer, while the Port gathers community feedback on seismic and flood protections for the next 75 years.
topStories.body:
The new public plaza at Fisherman's Wharf — built on the site of the demolished Alioto's Restaurant — is on track to open this summer as part of the Port of San Francisco's Fisherman's Wharf Forward initiative. Construction of the 5,000-square-foot plaza began in early March after the former restaurant building was demolished in February, and completion is targeted for this summer. The plaza will feature wave-shaped seating, picnic tables, views of the Golden Gate Bridge, and new lighting celebrating the historic fishing fleet in the inner lagoon.

Meanwhile, the Port has entered a new planning phase for the project's longer-term resilience goals. As reported by the SF Examiner in early May, the agency will spend the next several months gathering feedback from waterfront business owners, fishermen, and other stakeholders to shape seismic and flood-protection plans designed to protect the wharf through the year 2100.

Some community members have launched an effort to name the new plaza after the Alioto family, whose patriarch Nunzio Alioto opened a fish stand at the wharf 101 years ago. In addition to the plaza, new restaurant openings at the wharf are also in the pipeline: Oakland barbecue institution Everett & Jones has put up its sign at 300 Jefferson Street and is anticipated to open this summer, while a Salvadoran restaurant called Chasca Rio's is expected to open by the end of 2026 in the former Pompeii's Grotto space.

The improvements come as Fisherman's Wharf recorded one million visitors in March 2026 — a 24% increase over the same month in 2024, and the first time the neighborhood hit that mark since the pandemic.

topStories.headline: Original Joe's Suffers Kitchen Fire in North Beach — Its Third Blaze in Two Decades
topStories.dek: The flagship North Beach restaurant was evacuated on May 20 after a flue fire sent heavy smoke rising from the roof, but no injuries or displacements were reported.
topStories.body:
Firefighters quickly contained a one-alarm fire at the Original Joe's restaurant on the corner of Union and Stockton streets on the evening of Wednesday, May 20. The San Francisco Fire Department responded just after 8 p.m. after heavy smoke was spotted rising from the building's roof, and had the fire contained by 8:19 p.m.

According to SFFD spokesperson Samuel Menchaca, firefighters found heavy smoke coming from the roof upon arrival. The fire was reportedly in the kitchen's chimney or exhaust flue. The restaurant and a neighboring building were evacuated as a precaution, and portions of Union Street and Stockton Street were temporarily closed during the response.

The SFFD confirmed there were no injuries or displacements, and the building was turned back over to its owners that evening. As SFist noted, this marks the third fire in 20 years for the Original Joe's owners. The flagship North Beach location opened in 2012 after the original Taylor Street restaurant in the Tenderloin burned down in 2007. A newer Walnut Creek location also suffered a kitchen fire shortly after opening last year.

The fire came just two days after another small fire in North Beach on May 18, when SFFD extinguished a blaze on the roof of a building on the 400 block of Green Street. No one was displaced in that incident either.

topStories.headline: Verdi Building Demolition Dispute Heads to Board of Appeals as Neighbors Clash Over North Beach's Future
topStories.dek: The burned-out Verdi Building at 659 Union Street remains standing as preservationists, tenant advocates, and housing supporters battle over whether the historic structure should be demolished and what should replace it.
topStories.body:
The long-simmering dispute over the fate of the Verdi Building at 659 Union Street — a burned-out shell that has loomed over Washington Square Park since a 2018 fire — reached the San Francisco Board of Appeals this month. The North Beach Tenants Committee and the Telegraph Hill Dwellers both filed briefs supporting an appeal that halted emergency demolition of the structure in late March.

The appeal was filed after developer Red Bridge Partners, led by Jeff Jurow, obtained an emergency permit to demolish the entire building — even though the original 2023 permit only authorized removal of a 50-foot section of the rear wall. The North Beach Tenants Committee contends that the Verdi Building is eligible for national and state historic registers and that full demolition requires environmental review. The group also raised concerns that Red Bridge may be trying to circumvent commitments to reserve housing for tenants displaced by the 2018 fire.

Red Bridge's attorneys have argued that the demolition does not involve removing existing housing units and therefore does not trigger replacement housing requirements. District 3 Supervisor Danny Sauter, while supporting the eventual redevelopment of the site, expressed frustration that the appeal process has left a dangerous structure standing even longer.

The developer's current plans call for an eight-story, 96-foot-high building with 89 apartments, a rooftop restaurant, and ground-floor retail. The Telegraph Hill Dwellers have raised concerns that the new structure could cast shadows on Washington Square Park in violation of Proposition K, the voter-approved 1984 shadow ban.

events.title: Festa Italiana Street Fair
events.location: Washington Square Park & SF Italian Athletic Club, 1630 Stockton St., North Beach
events.description: San Francisco's only Italian street festival returns with Italian marionette shows, tarantella dancing, the famous pizza toss, live Italian music, an accordion symphony, and an Italian marketplace.

events.title: 72nd Annual North Beach Festival
events.location: Columbus Ave. & Green St., North Beach (spanning 11 blocks between Filbert & Broadway, Stockton & Grant)
events.description: One of the country's original outdoor festivals, featuring 200+ vendors, live music on three stages, beer gardens, Italian street painting, a Blessing of the Animals at 1 PM, interactive art activities, and a kids' Fun Zone. Free admission. Presented by the North Beach Business Association.

events.title: 'Dear San Francisco' at Club Fugazi
events.location: Club Fugazi, 678 Green St., North Beach
events.description: The critically-acclaimed immersive circus show from The 7 Fingers, now in its fifth year, takes audiences on an acrobatic journey through San Francisco's history. Tickets from $65.

events.title: Fisherman's Wharf Weekly Pop-Up Fish Market
events.description: Buy locally caught seafood — including salmon, black cod, rockfish, and halibut — directly from San Francisco fishers at this weekly pop-up market operated by the local fishing community with support from the Port and the Fisherman's Wharf Community Benefit District.

events.title: Chonkers the Sea Lion at Pier 39
events.description: A massive 2,000-pound Steller sea lion nicknamed 'Chonkers' continues to draw crowds at the famous Pier 39 sea lion docks, where he towers over the smaller California sea lions. Check the Pier 39 live cam before visiting.

events.title: Critical Mass Bike Ride
events.description: The monthly mass bicycle ride departs from Embarcadero Plaza on the last Friday of each month. All are welcome; route is spontaneous.

moreNews.headline: Buena Vista Bartender Joseph Shaw Celebrates 50 Years of Pouring Irish Coffees
moreNews.body: Joseph Shaw, 72, the longest-serving bartender at The Buena Vista near Fisherman's Wharf, celebrated 50 years behind the bar on May 15. Shaw started as a barback in 1976 and marked the occasion by pouring a ceremonial row of 50 Irish coffees for friends and longtime customers. He received an official resolution and certificate of honor from the Board of Supervisors during the celebration. Shaw told KTVU he plans to continue working at the iconic bar, though he intends to scale back to part-time hours.

moreNews.headline: Fisherman's Wharf Fishermen Raise Concerns Over Redevelopment's Impact on Working Waterfront
moreNews.body: As the Fisherman's Wharf Forward project moves ahead, commercial fishermen are voicing concerns that working waterfront needs are being pushed behind tourism and public-space development, as reported by National Fisherman. Fisherman Sal Alioto pointed to the loss of the wharf's longtime machine shop and boatworking facilities, while industry leaders flagged outdated ice-making equipment critical for preserving seafood quality. The Port says it is collaborating with local fishers on harbor enhancements, but many in the fleet are watching closely to ensure their livelihoods are not sidelined.

moreNews.headline: New Fisherman's Wharf Entertainment Zone Approved
moreNews.body: The Fisherman's Wharf Community Benefit District's Entertainment Zone — a city designation allowing open-air alcohol consumption — has been officially approved. The zone spans from Polk Street to Powell Street between Beach Street and the Little Embarcadero. According to the March 2026 Fisherman's Wharf Advisory Committee meeting minutes, the district planned to launch the zone's first event with an annual chowder competition in May.
